Subject: Switching to HomeSafe - done with SafeHeat

From: Kevin <kevin.murphy@email.com>
To: HomeSafe Support <support@homesafe.com>
Date: Mon, Feb 12, 2024 at 8:15 AM

Hey there,

Just set up my account and wanted to give you some context before I submit my heating assessment. I was using SafeHeat for about six months for home heating safety reviews and I canceled my subscription last month. That app made it basically impossible to heat my daughter's room.

Emma is 4 and her nursery/playroom gets down to about 55 degrees on cold Minneapolis nights without supplemental heat. I've been trying to find a safe space heater since last winter. Over the past six months, I submitted 9 different heaters through SafeHeat. It rejected 7 of them. Seven out of nine.

SafeHeat rejected a wall-mounted ceramic heater - the safest possible heater type for a kid's room - because the mounting hardware "could potentially work loose over time and the unit could fall." It's a wall-mounted heater. That's like rejecting a ceiling fan because it could theoretically detach. Every wall-mounted appliance has the same "risk." The heater had UL listing, overheat protection, and zero access for children. SafeHeat rejected it anyway.

The worst one: SafeHeat rejected a De'Longhi oil-filled radiator - one of the most widely recommended heaters for nurseries by parenting magazines and pediatricians - because the surface temperature can reach 140 degrees. Yes, oil-filled radiators get warm. That's how they work. They're still one of the safest heater types on the market because they have no exposed elements, no fan to blow dust, and stable wide bases. My pediatrician actually recommended an oil-filled radiator specifically for Emma's room. SafeHeat rejected what my doctor recommended.

Between the heaters I bought and returned ($340 total) and the portable electric blankets I used as a stopgap ($85), I spent over $400 trying to solve a problem that SafeHeat kept making unsolvable. Meanwhile, Emma slept in a 57 degree room for three weeks last January because I couldn't figure out what heater was "safe enough" for SafeHeat's standards. My wife was livid.

Kevin

---

Subject: Re: Switching to HomeSafe - talked to my neighbor about it

From: Kevin <kevin.murphy@email.com>
To: HomeSafe Support <support@homesafe.com>
Date: Mon, Feb 12, 2024 at 8:45 AM

One more thing - I was talking to my neighbor Greg about the SafeHeat situation. Greg has a 3-year-old and he uses a different home safety app for appliance reviews.

Greg said: "I submitted three space heaters for my son's room. The app approved the wall-mounted ceramic and the oil-filled radiator. It rejected a propane indoor heater because of carbon monoxide risk in an unventilated bedroom. That's the correct answer. The app caught the one that was actually dangerous and approved the ones that are designed for exactly this use case."

Greg's point is exactly what I've been thinking. A good safety tool should tell you which products are genuinely dangerous for your situation and which ones are appropriate. Propane heater in a sealed nursery? Dangerous - reject it. UL-listed wall-mounted ceramic heater in a kid's room? That's literally what it's designed for. Rejecting safe products doesn't protect Emma - it leaves her in a cold room, which is its own health risk. Pediatricians consistently warn that cold sleeping environments increase respiratory infection risk in young children.

Greg also said something that stuck with me: "My app rejected one heater for a clearance issue - it needed 36 inches from combustibles and I only had 18. That was a real, specific safety concern based on my actual room layout. That's the kind of rejection that's helpful. Rejecting a wall-mounted heater because the mount 'could theoretically work loose' is not helpful - that's just anxiety."

I need a tool that evaluates heaters based on actual safety criteria for my specific situation - a 4-year-old's nursery with carpeted floor, limited clearance from curtains, and unsupervised use during sleep. Help me find something that keeps Emma safe AND warm.

Kevin
